perturbation.original_text: The deposit accounts of the Bank are insured by the FDIC up to the applicable limits; and no proceedings for the termination or revocation of such insurance are pending or, to the best knowledge of the Company or the Bank, threatened.
perturbation.changed_text: The deposit accounts of the Bank may be insured; proceedings for the termination or revocation of such insurance are not, to the best knowledge of the Company or the Bank, threatened.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the phrase 'up to the applicable limits' suggests that insurance is not guaranteed or may not fully cover deposits, contradicting FDIC regulations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 12 U.S. Code § 1821 - Insurance funds; insured status
perturbation.law_explanation: Federal law mandates deposit insurance up to $250,000 per depositor. Removing 'up to the applicable limits' implies the bank might not adhere to these legally required insurance limits, potentially misleading depositors about the extent of their coverage.

(1/4/1995) Back to Original Document << Previous TITLE 12 / CHAPTER 16 / § 1821 Next >> [Print] [Print selection] [OLRC Home] Help 12 USC 1821 : Insurance Funds Text contains those laws in effect on June 14, 2025 From Title 12-BANKS AND BANKING CHAPTER 16-FEDERAL DEPOSIT INSURANCE CORPORATION Jump To: Source Credit Miscellaneous References In Text Codification Prior Provisions Amendments Change of Name Effective Date Regulations Cross Reference §1821. Insurance Funds (a) Deposit insurance (1) Insured amounts payable (A) In general The Corporation shall insure the deposits of all insured depository institutions as provided in this chapter. (B) Net amount of insured deposit The net amount due to any depositor at an insured depository institution shall not exceed the standard maximum deposit insurance amount as determined in accordance
perturbation.justification: The original text states that deposit accounts are insured by the FDIC 'up to the applicable limits'. The changed text omits this phrase, suggesting that the insurance might not be limited, or potentially not fully cover the deposits. This omission creates a legal contradiction because it undermines the legally mandated consumer protection of deposit insurance up to a specific limit, as described in the provided law explanation. 12 USC 1821(a)(1)(B) states that 'The net amount due to any depositor at an insured depository institution shall not exceed the standard maximum deposit insurance amount as determined in accordance'. By removing the phrase, the contract implies a potentially unlimited or non-existent insurance limit, contradicting the federal law requiring a standard maximum deposit insurance amount. This constitutes an Omission Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: In order to provide for just and equitable contribution in circumstances in which the indemnification provided for in Section 8 is due in accordance with its terms but is for any reason held by a court to be unavailable from the Company, the MHC, the Bank or the Agent, the Company, the MHC, the Bank and the Agent shall contribute to the aggregate losses, claims, damages and liabilities (including any investigation, legal and other expenses incurred in connection with, and any amount paid in settlement of, any action, suit or proceeding of any claims asserted, but after deducting any contribution received by the Company, the MHC, the Bank or the Agent from persons other than the other party thereto, who may also be liable for contribution) in such proportion so that the Agent is responsible for that portion represented by the percentage that the fees paid to the Agent pursuant to Section 2 of this Agreement (not including expenses) bears to the gross proceeds received by the Company from the sale of the Shares in the Offering, and the Company, the MHC and the Bank shall be responsible for the balance.
perturbation.changed_text: In circumstances in which the indemnification provided for in Section 8 is held by a court to be unavailable from the Company, the MHC, the Bank or the Agent, the Company, the MHC, the Bank and the Agent may contribute to the aggregate losses, claims, damages and liabilities. The final decision will be made at the discretion of the company.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the specific formula for contribution and replacing it with a discretionary decision clause removes a key aspect of the contribution agreement. The agent contribution may not be given to losses incurred.
perturbation.contradicted_law: American Law Institute, Restatement of the Law Second, Torts § 886B
perturbation.law_explanation: The Restatement of Torts § 886B outlines principles of contribution among tortfeasors. By making contribution discretionary and not based on a fair formula, the modified text contradicts the principle that joint tortfeasors should equitably share liability based on their degree of fault.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: contribution | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you contribution Contribution is an important term in the fields of business and tort law . Tort Law In the field of tort law, contribution refers to an action a defendant may bring in a joint and several liability jurisdiction to recover for damages they paid out but did not cause. In a jurisdiction that follows joint and several liability, a negligent defendant is liable to pay damages for all harm suffered by the plaintiff , even if the negligence of other parties caused some of that harm. After a plaintiff collects from a jointly and severally liable defendant , that defendant may initiate a separate lawsuit seeking contribution against the co-defendants . In this suit, the co-defendants will be required to pay damages in proportion to their blame for causing the injuries. For example, suppose that A and B negligently injure V. Suppose further that V successfully sues A and B for $100,000, that the jury finds A 10% responsible for V's injuries, and B 90% responsible. Because A and B are jointly and severally liable , V may collect the full $100,000 from A. A may then demand that B pay their share of the judgment - here, $90,000. Ideally, allowing for contribution means that the ultimate outcome of a lawsuit in joint and several liability jurisdictions is the same as one in a several liability jurisdiction. In practice, however, contribution shifts the risk of insolvency from plaintiffs to defendants. Imagine again the scenario above where A is 10% responsible and B is 90% responsible. In a several liability jurisdiction, where negligent defendants are only liable for their proportionate share of damages, V can collect at most $10,000 from A and $90,000 from B. In the event that B is insolvent, the most V can collect is $10,000. In a joint and several liability jurisdiction, V will still collect $100,000 if B is insolvent but A will lose an extra $90,000 because they can no longer obtain contribution. For an example of this concept in practice, see Walt Disney World v. Wood . Business Law In business law, contribution may refer to a capital contribution, which is money or assets given to a business or partnership by one of the owners or partners . The capital contribution increases the owner or partner's equity interest in the entity. Capital contributions are not considered business income unless given in the form of a loan. Contribution may also refer to a charitable contribution , which is money or assets given to a corporation organized and operated exclusively for religious, charitable, scientific, literary, or educational purposes. Charitable contributions are tax deductible for up to one half of an individual's adjusted gross income , and up to ten percent of a corporation's taxable income. [Last reviewed in July of 2022 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Keywords llcs-corporations-partnerships Wex accidents and injuries CIVICS taxation COMMERCE commercial activities accounting finance financial services LIFE EVENTS accidents & injuries (tort law) tort damages THE LEGAL PROCESS legal practice/ethics wex articles business law business organizations contracts corporations mergers & acquisitions wex definitions business sectors civil procedure commercial transactions legal education and practice money and financial problems Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The original contract text provides a specific formula for contribution among the involved parties. The changed text removes this formula and replaces it with a discretionary clause, allowing the company to decide on contribution. The Perturbation Explanation notes that this removes a key aspect of the contribution agreement, and the Law Explanation mentions the principle that joint tortfeasors should equitably share liability. Scraped Law Snippet 2 defines contribution in tort law as an action a defendant may bring to recover for damages they paid out but did not cause, and specifies that co-defendants will be required to pay damages in proportion to their blame for causing the injuries. The changed text's discretionary nature contradicts this principle of proportionate contribution, making it likely to fail legal scrutiny and creating a clear non-compliance. Therefore, a legal contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Kansas.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be construed in accordance with the laws of a jurisdiction chosen by the Company.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the specification of Kansas law and making the jurisdiction selection discretionary removes legal predictability for the agent. By choosing the place of jurisdiction, it could remove consumer rights to benefit the company.
perturbation.law_explanation: While parties can agree to a forum selection clause, courts consider fairness and reasonableness when enforcing them. Allowing the Company to unilaterally choose the jurisdiction *after* a dispute arises could be deemed unfair, especially if the chosen jurisdiction lacks consumer protections available in the agent's jurisdiction. This contradicts the principles of fair venue and access to justice.

Change of venue (a) For the convenience of parties and witnesses, in the interest of justice, a district court may transfer any civil action to any other district or division where it might have been brought or to any district or division to which all parties have consented. (b) Upon motion, consent or stipulation of all parties, any action, suit or proceeding of a civil nature or any motion or hearing thereof, may be transferred, in t
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the specification that Kansas law governs the agreement, instead granting the company the discretion to choose the jurisdiction. The Law Explanation says that this contradicts 'principles of fair venue and access to justice' if the selected jurisdiction lacks the consumer protections available in the agent's jurisdiction. This aligns with the Omission Legal Contradiction definition, as the contract omits a legally mandated consumer protection by allowing the company to choose a jurisdiction potentially detrimental to the agent. 28 USC 1404, provided as a snippet, addresses the 'change of venue,' suggesting that while venue can be altered, it must be 'in the interest of justice' (subsection a) and with consent/stipulation of all parties. The change in the contract potentially circumvents these principles by allowing unilateral company selection, possibly undermining the agent's access to justice.
